headline: Beavers Are Reshaping Coastal Ecosystems in the Pacific Northwest
meta_description: New research reveals beavers are far more common in estuaries than previously known, engineering critical habitat for endangered salmon species.
summary_short: A groundbreaking study finds beavers thriving in tidal wetlands across the Pacific Northwest, creating vital refuges for salmon at low tide.
body:
Greg Hood had an unconventional career pivot. The estuarine ecologist describes himself as an "accidental beaver biologist" after discovering something surprising in coastal habitats: beavers are everywhere—and no one had really noticed.

Hood surveyed estuaries and tidal wetlands across British Columbia, Washington, and Oregon, finding beavers in places other scientists typically overlooked. The reason was simple: tidal swamps are "very hard to move around in," so researchers rarely ventured there. When Hood did, he discovered beaver dams at twice the density in some tidal channels of the Snohomish and Skagit rivers compared to non-tidal rivers.

These coastal beavers engineer differently than their inland cousins. Their dams are shorter, likely designed to trap water during low tides so the rodents can move freely when the water recedes. This adaptation has profound ecological consequences: the dams create crucial low-tide refuges for fish species, potentially including endangered Puget Sound Chinook salmon and Oregon Coast coho salmon.

Hood and beaver biologists from the Tulalip Tribes are now taking the work further, tagging beavers with GPS trackers to understand their movements and habitat needs in these complex tidal ecosystems. As environmental journalist Ben Goldfarb notes, acknowledging these coastal beavers' importance might be vital to restoring lost intertidal worlds—ecosystems shaped by rodent teeth, and now being restored by them.
